stories.headline: Avalanche sweep weekend with shootout win over Wild as Kadri, Roy contribute in debuts
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche defeated the Minnesota Wild 3-2 in a shootout Sunday at Ball Arena, sweeping a four-point weekend after also beating Dallas in a shootout Friday night. Nathan MacKinnon and Valeri Nichushkin scored in the shootout, with goaltender Scott Wedgewood stopping 32 shots and three more in the one-on-one competition. The wins pushed Colorado nine points ahead of Dallas and 10 ahead of Minnesota in the Central Division standings, putting the division title and Western Conference's top seed within clear reach.

Newly acquired forward Nazem Kadri recorded an assist in his return to Denver, setting up MacKinnon's 43rd goal of the season with a heads-up read on a Wild defenseman's drop pass. Nicolas Roy, acquired Thursday in a trade with Toronto, tipped in the tying goal at 12:39 of the third period to force overtime. Both newcomers drew loud ovations from the Ball Arena crowd.

The Avalanche's special teams struggled, going 0-for-5 on the power play and surrendering a shorthanded goal — Colorado's 11th allowed this season, a league high. Minnesota's Kirill Kaprizov scored on the power play and Nico Sturm added a shorthanded tally to tie the game before the shootout decided it. Colorado improved to 34-0-0 when leading after two periods.

Kadri expressed confidence in the team's Cup potential after his first game back, drawing comparisons to the 2022 championship run. MacKinnon's smile on the ice during their reunion moment drew notice from fans and teammates alike, with many wearing vintage Kadri jerseys in the stands.

stories.headline: Avalanche captain Gabe Landeskog out week-to-week with lower-body injury
stories.summary:
Avalanche captain Gabe Landeskog will not play in the near term after sustaining a lower-body injury during Friday's win against the Dallas Stars, the team confirmed Sunday. Landeskog took a Cale Makar shot to the groin area during the game and is listed as week-to-week. He displayed a puck-dented athletic cup to reporters after the game.

The injury is a setback for a player who has battled significant health issues in recent seasons, having missed three consecutive regular seasons with knee problems before returning this year. Landeskog had posted nine goals and 29 points in 47 games this season after also missing 14 games earlier in the year with an upper-body injury, before returning for Sweden at the 2026 Winter Olympics.

The timing is notable given the Avalanche's playoff push and the return of Nazem Kadri, another member of the 2022 Stanley Cup championship core. The team will continue to monitor Landeskog's progress as they pursue the Central Division title and top seed in the Western Conference.

stories.headline: Broncos re-sign inside linebacker Justin Strnad to three-year, $18 million deal
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos have agreed to terms with inside linebacker Justin Strnad on a three-year contract worth $18 million, with $10 million guaranteed at signing, multiple sources confirmed to The Denver Post. The deal secures one of the team's breakout defensive contributors ahead of the NFL free agency period. Strnad, a fifth-round pick in 2020, recorded 58 tackles, 4.5 sacks and an interception in 17 games during the 2025 season.

General manager George Paton had signaled at the NFL Combine that the team was committed to retaining Strnad and fellow linebacker Alex Singleton. The re-signing of Strnad, at a salary reflecting starter-level value, suggests Denver may move on from either Singleton or veteran Dre Greenlaw, whose release would free $6 million in cap space. Strnad had said publicly after the season that he would only return in a starting role.

Strnad's career arc has been unusual — he played as a spot starter as a rookie in 2021, spent two full seasons without a single defensive snap in 2022 and 2023, then emerged as a key piece of Vance Joseph's top-three defense over the last two seasons. Singleton praised Strnad as deserving of a starter's contract, calling him as good as the top-paid linebackers in the league.

stories.headline: Skier killed in avalanche near Copper Mountain; more than 40 slides reported across Colorado mountains
stories.summary:
A skier was found dead Sunday morning in avalanche debris in the Boss Basin area south of Ptarmigan Pass, a few miles west of Copper Mountain, according to the Colorado Avalanche Information Center. The person had been reported missing Saturday evening while riding a snowmobile, and search and rescue teams from Summit County, Vail Mountain Rescue and both Summit and Eagle County sheriff's offices responded overnight. The victim was discovered at approximately sunrise, buried in a two-foot-deep avalanche with ski and snowmobile tracks leading into the debris field.

The fatal slide came after significant late-week mountain snowfall across the region. Spotters near the site reported 6 to 8 inches of fresh snow by Saturday morning, while other areas saw far greater accumulations — more than 23 inches in the Indian Peaks Wilderness, 18 inches at Berthoud Pass, 15 inches at Longs Peak and 12 inches at Loveland Pass.

More than 40 backcountry users reported observing avalanches across Colorado's mountain ranges between Friday and Sunday, with at least one person caught in a slide and 12 others reporting triggering slides. No additional injuries had been reported as of Sunday afternoon. Officials extended condolences to the victim's family as the avalanche remained under investigation.

stories.headline: NTSB: Plane that killed four in northern Colorado mountains was flying too low on approach not authorized for night use
stories.summary:
A federal preliminary investigation has found that a small plane that crashed into Emerald Mountain near Steamboat Springs in February, killing all four people aboard, was using an approach not authorized for night operations while flying well below the minimum required altitude. The Epic E1000 aircraft, carrying Tennessee residents Aaron Stokes, Jakson Stokes, Colin Stokes and Austin Huskey, struck the 8,250-foot peak on Feb. 13 after departing Nashville and stopping for fuel in Kansas City. The plane's last recorded altitude was 8,221 feet — nearly 900 feet below the 9,100-foot minimum descent altitude for the approach in use.

According to the National Transportation Safety Board's preliminary report published Feb. 28, air traffic controllers provided the pilot with approach information and then switched radio frequencies after the pilot referenced the RNAV 32 approach. The pilot acknowledged the change but sent no further communications, prompting controllers to issue an alert notice. Flight data showed the aircraft was operating normally and flying on autopilot at the time of impact.

Six days after the crash, the Federal Aviation Administration issued a notice prohibiting pilots from using the RNAV 32 approach into Steamboat Springs, with a "Procedure NA" designation in effect through October. The NTSB will publish a final report including probable cause and contributing factors within two years.

stories.headline: Fire weather risk to build along Front Range later this week as warm, sunny start gives way to strong winds
stories.summary:
Sunny skies and mild temperatures are expected to open the week across the Denver metro area, with highs reaching the upper 50s to near 60 degrees Sunday and Monday, according to the National Weather Service in Denver and Boulder. Breezy conditions will develop near the mountains and foothills, with a cooler and windier pattern expected to arrive by Tuesday afternoon. A chance of rain or snow showers is possible Tuesday night into early Wednesday, particularly in mountain areas.

Conditions are forecast to shift more significantly by Thursday and Friday, when strong winds combined with dry air could create critical fire weather conditions across portions of eastern Colorado and the Front Range. Officials are encouraging residents to exercise caution with outdoor burning and activities that could spark a wildfire during periods of elevated fire danger. The National Weather Service urged residents to monitor updated forecasts and fire weather alerts as the week progresses.

stories.headline: Nearly 100 evacuated from Centennial Extended Stay hotel after room fire Sunday afternoon
stories.summary:
Firefighters and Arapahoe County Sheriff's deputies responded to a fire at the Extended Stay America Select Suites at 9604 E. Easter Ave. in Centennial shortly before 2:30 p.m. Sunday, according to South Metro Fire Rescue. Nearly 100 guests were evacuated from the property, and the blaze was contained to the room of origin and brought under control within 30 minutes. Occupants were relocated to a different hotel.

Paramedics transported one person to a hospital for further evaluation, but no other injuries were reported. The cause of the fire had not been determined as of Sunday evening. Photos released by the fire department showed broken windows with curtains hanging from the room where the fire originated.

stories.headline: Colorado lawmakers weigh $1 billion mental health funding gap as state ranks among nation's worst for outcomes
stories.summary:
Colorado legislators are confronting a mental health funding crisis tied to a budget shortfall exceeding $1 billion, with multiple proposals this legislative session aimed at improving services in a state that consistently ranks among the worst in the nation for mental health outcomes. The scope of the shortfall is forcing difficult choices about which programs to sustain or expand. Advocates and health officials have pressed lawmakers to prioritize mental health amid competing budget demands.

Several bills are under consideration this session, though specific proposals and their current status in the legislature were not immediately detailed. The challenge reflects both the scale of unmet need in Colorado communities and the structural constraints on state spending. Mental health has emerged as a recurring legislative priority in recent sessions as Colorado grapples with high rates of suicide, substance use and access barriers.

stories.headline: MSU Denver backs House Bill 26-1317 to unify Colorado's postsecondary education and workforce system
stories.summary:
Metropolitan State University of Denver has publicly endorsed House Bill 26-1317, legislation that would consolidate oversight of Colorado's fragmented higher education and workforce development systems under the Department of Higher Education. The bill would create an advisory committee to develop a transition plan by November, with a unified department operational by July 2028. Currently, more than 20 divisions across seven state entities administer more than 100 programs, creating navigation challenges for students and job seekers.

Proponents argue the reform is needed given projections that nearly 73 percent of Colorado jobs will require a postsecondary credential by 2031. The proposed committee would include representatives from higher education, workforce development, business, labor and apprenticeship programs. Supporters say the goal is reducing duplication and improving alignment rather than expanding bureaucracy.

MSU Denver, which describes itself as Colorado's top school for social mobility, cited its own model as evidence of the connection between education and employment outcomes. The university noted that nearly 90 percent of its students work while earning degrees and that 3,000 students annually participate in work-based learning through partnerships with more than 5,700 industry employers.

weather.notable: About 19 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: Home Depot is amazing. You walk in thinking you're going to fix a faucet and walk out $300 poorer with a grill.

topStory.headline: Iran Names Mojtaba Khamenei as New Supreme Leader After Father Killed in U.S.-Israeli Strikes
topStory.summary:
Iran's Assembly of Experts on Saturday named Mojtaba Khamenei, the 56-year-old son of slain Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei, as the third supreme leader of the Islamic Republic. The elder Khamenei was killed on February 28 in joint U.S.-Israeli airstrikes that launched the current conflict. The Assembly declared that Mojtaba had been selected "by a decisive vote," though reports from Iran International indicated that Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps commanders pressured Assembly members into the selection, with those who argued against the appointment given limited time to speak before discussion was cut off.

Mojtaba Khamenei is widely regarded as a hardline figure with deep ties to the IRGC. A mid-ranking cleric who has never given a public speech and has long operated behind the scenes, his appointment marks the first time leadership of the Islamic Republic has passed from father to son -- a striking development for a revolutionary system founded on the explicit rejection of hereditary monarchy. His father was reportedly opposed to the idea of dynastic succession, fearing it would undermine the republic's founding principles.

The appointment drew swift international reaction. President Trump, who had previously called Mojtaba a "lightweight," said he was "not happy" with the selection and that any leader named without U.S. approval would "not last long." Israel's military warned that any successor to the late Khamenei would be considered a target. Russia and China, meanwhile, voiced support for the new leader, with President Putin pledging "unwavering" backing.

Analysts say the selection of Mojtaba Khamenei signals that Iran's hardline establishment intends to maintain its current course rather than pursue negotiations as the U.S.-Israeli military campaign enters its second week. Oil prices surged past $100 per barrel for the first time since 2022, with Brent crude reaching $118, and global stock markets tumbled on Monday as the geopolitical crisis deepened.
